pattern: Once every value has grown to sit within one multiplier factor of the others, the operation cycles through the same indices in the same fixed order forever, so only a short prefix needs literal simulation before the rest is pure counting.
hints: You cannot literally repeat the operation k times when k is up to 10^9 - think about what happens to the relative order of the array once every value has caught up to roughly the same magnitude as its neighbors.

Simulate with a min-heap only until the sequence of chosen indices starts repeating identically every n operations; once that happens the exact same order repeats forever, because multiplying every value by the same positive number never changes their relative order.

Once you know how many full repeating cycles remain and which prefix of that cycle gets one extra operation, add up each element's total multiplication count and finish with modular exponentiation - never build the astronomically large intermediate value directly.

edge_inputs.description: single element repeatedly multiplied, small k
all-tied values, ties always broken by first index
duplicate minimums scattered through the array
multiplier=1 is a no-op regardless of k
value near the upper bound with a large multiplier, exercising the modulo arithmetic
large gap between smallest and largest values stresses heap ordering
k far larger than the array needs the repeating-cycle shortcut, not literal simulation, but is still small enough here for the oracle to double-check exactly
minimum shifts between two tied positions across several rounds

stress.description: near-max array length with k up to the full 10^9 bound
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = max(1, size)
    nums = [rng.randint(1, 1000000000) for _ in range(n)]
    k = rng.randint(1, 1000000000)
    multiplier = rng.randint(1, 1000000)
    return [nums, k, multiplier]

stress.description: very small array with the maximal k, deep into the repeating-cycle shortcut
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = max(1, min(size, 6))
    nums = [rng.randint(1, 1000000000) for _ in range(n)]
    k = 1000000000
    multiplier = rng.randint(2, 1000000)
    return [nums, k, multiplier]

oracle_python:
import heapq


class Solution:
    def getFinalState(self, nums, k, multiplier):
        MOD = 10 ** 9 + 7
        if multiplier == 1:
            return [x % MOD for x in nums]
        nums = nums[:]
        heap = [(v, i) for i, v in enumerate(nums)]
        heapq.heapify(heap)
        for _ in range(k):
            v, i = heapq.heappop(heap)
            nums[i] = v * multiplier
            heapq.heappush(heap, (nums[i], i))
        return [x % MOD for x in nums]

solutions.python:
import heapq


class Solution:
    def getFinalState(self, nums, k, multiplier):
        MOD = 10 ** 9 + 7
        n = len(nums)
        if multiplier == 1 or k <= 0:
            return [x % MOD for x in nums]
        vals = nums[:]
        opcount = [0] * n
        heap = [(vals[i], i) for i in range(n)]
        heapq.heapify(heap)

        ops_done = 0
        cap_ramp = min(k, n * 80 + 200)
        history = []
        periodic_order = None
        full_set = set(range(n))

        while ops_done < cap_ramp:
            v, i = heapq.heappop(heap)
            vals[i] = v * multiplier
            opcount[i] += 1
            ops_done += 1
            heapq.heappush(heap, (vals[i], i))
            history.append(i)
            if len(history) >= 2 * n:
                last = history[-n:]
                prev = history[-2 * n:-n]
                if last == prev and set(last) == full_set:
                    periodic_order = last
                    break

        if periodic_order is not None:
            remaining = k - ops_done
            full_cycles, leftover = divmod(remaining, n)
            for i in range(n):
                opcount[i] += full_cycles
            for idx in periodic_order[:leftover]:
                opcount[idx] += 1

        return [(nums[i] * pow(multiplier, opcount[i], MOD)) % MOD for i in range(n)]

complexity.time: O((n + m) log n) where m is a small bound on the ramp-up before the cycle repeats
